title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe how they have worked on developing themselves. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Better Every Day: Four People on Personal Growth and Self-Improvement

texts.A:
### Elena, 29, Marketing Executive
[43]I used to be the kind of person who blamed external circumstances for everything that went wrong in my life.[/43] It was only after a particularly honest conversation with a mentor that I started to take responsibility for my own choices. I began journalling every morning – not to record what happened, but to question my reactions and assumptions. [47]Reading books about cognitive behavioural therapy was a turning point; I learned to notice unhelpful thought patterns before they took hold.[/47] [51]I still slip into old habits occasionally, but I now catch myself much faster and find it easier to redirect my thinking.[/51]
texts.B:
### Raj, 41, Accountant
[44]For me, personal growth began the day I admitted to a close friend that I was deeply unhappy despite what looked from the outside like a successful life.[/44] I had a good job, a nice home, and no obvious problems, yet I felt stuck. That conversation prompted me to see a therapist, which I had previously dismissed as something only people in crisis needed. [48]Therapy taught me that many of the beliefs limiting my potential had been shaped by experiences in early childhood that I had never properly examined.[/48] I am more self-aware now than I have ever been, though the process is ongoing and sometimes uncomfortable.
texts.C:
### Fiona, 36, Physiotherapist
[45]I signed up for an online leadership course two years ago mainly because my employer offered to pay for it, but it ended up changing the way I see myself.[/45] I had never thought of myself as a leader, but the course encouraged me to reframe the skills I already had as strengths rather than just personality traits. [49]The most challenging exercise involved asking five colleagues to give me honest feedback, and hearing things I hadn't expected gave me a lot to think about.[/49] [52]Since completing the course, I have taken on a supervisory role at work that I would never have applied for previously.[/52] Growth, I've come to believe, rarely feels comfortable while it's happening.
texts.D:
### Henry, 24, Recent Graduate
[46]After graduating, I spent several months feeling directionless and comparing myself constantly to friends who seemed to have everything figured out.[/46] I eventually decided to stop scrolling through social media and redirect that time into something constructive. I started waking up an hour earlier each day and using that time to learn Spanish and practise guitar. [50]What I didn't expect was how building discipline in one area would spill over into other parts of my life – I became more focused at work, more patient in relationships, and generally less anxious.[/50] Small habits, I've found, have the power to reshape your whole outlook.

questions.question: used to hold other people or situations responsible for their problems?
questions.explanation: Elena describes herself as someone who blamed external circumstances for everything that went wrong before a mentor prompted her to change.

questions.question: began their journey of self-improvement by confiding in someone close to them?
questions.explanation: Raj says personal growth began the day he admitted to a close friend that he was unhappy despite appearing successful.

questions.question: took part in a course without high expectations that ended up having a significant impact?
questions.explanation: Fiona signed up for a leadership course mainly because her employer covered the cost, but it changed the way she saw herself.

questions.question: felt lost and made unfavourable comparisons between themselves and their peers?
questions.explanation: Henry spent months feeling directionless after graduating and constantly comparing himself to friends who seemed to have life sorted out.

questions.question: describes learning to identify and interrupt negative patterns of thinking?
questions.explanation: Elena read books on cognitive behavioural therapy and learned to notice unhelpful thought patterns before they took hold.

questions.question: discovered that past experiences were responsible for beliefs that had been holding them back?
questions.explanation: Therapy revealed to Raj that many of his limiting beliefs had been shaped by childhood experiences he had never properly examined.

questions.question: found receiving candid feedback from others particularly challenging?
questions.explanation: Fiona describes an exercise where she asked five colleagues for honest feedback, and hearing unexpected things gave her a lot to think about.

questions.question: was surprised to find that improving in one aspect of life had a positive effect on other areas?
questions.explanation: Henry didn't expect that building discipline through morning habits would make him more focused at work and more patient in relationships.

questions.question: admits they have not completely overcome old tendencies but have developed better self-awareness?
questions.explanation: Elena says she still slips into old habits occasionally but now catches herself much faster.

questions.question: credits a self-development experience with enabling them to take on greater responsibility at work?
questions.explanation: Since completing the leadership course, Fiona took on a supervisory role she would never have applied for before.
